facts: A sharp knife needs less force and gives you more control than a dull one.
Resting meat lets its juices redistribute before slicing.
Acid can brighten a finished dish without adding more salt.
Toasting dry spices briefly can make their aromas more noticeable.
Pasta water helps sauces cling because it contains released starch.
Frozen vegetables are often packed soon after harvest.
Cast iron keeps heat well, which makes it useful for steady searing.
Salt added to boiling water seasons pasta from the inside.
Room-temperature citrus releases more juice than refrigerated citrus.
A damp towel under a cutting board helps keep it from sliding.
Beans provide both fiber and plant-based protein.
Letting dough rest gives flour time to hydrate more evenly.
Browning creates new flavors through the Maillard reaction.
Fresh herbs are usually best added near the end of cooking.
Labeling leftovers with a date makes meal planning much easier.
Wooden spoons do not conduct heat the way metal utensils do.
A pinch of salt can make naturally sweet foods taste sweeter.
Cooling rice promptly helps preserve its texture for later meals.
Cutting ingredients to similar sizes helps them cook evenly.
A thermometer removes the guesswork from checking meat for doneness.
